coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride modified with trace-level copper and carbon dots exhibits excellent photo-Fenton catalytic activity with low consumption of H2O2
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Lingyun
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Li, Ning
Hao, Caihong
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a competent candidate for heterogeneous Fenton catalysis application. However, g-C3N4 based heterogeneous Fenton catalysts suffer from either high consumption of H2O2 or inefficient catalytic efficiency. In this work, g-C3N4 modified with trace-level copper and carbon dots (CDs) was designed. Contrary to previous understanding, the results revealed that the introduction of Cu decreased the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 owing to the trapping of photogenerated charge carriers by introduced Cu species. But delightedly, the g-C3N4 modified with trace-level Cu (Cu-g-C3N4) not only exhibited exceptional Fenton-like catalytic activity with low consumption of H2O2 (4 mM), but also showed favorable photo-Fenton synergistic catalytic activity. The excellent performance was fundamentally due to the introduced trace-level Cu that avoided producing excessive defects acting as trapping centers of active radicals and simultaneously reduced the consumption of photogenerated charge carriers by Cu species themselves, giving rise to more active radicals and highly efficient utilization of H2O2. Moreover, after introducing CDs, the obtained CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed higher photo-Fenton activity in degrading different organic pollutants compared to Cu-g-C3N4 owing to the role of CDs in facilitating the charge transfer and strengthening the interaction with organic pollutants. The constructed Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed wide application pH and high stability, holding great potential in practical pollutants degradation applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-Fenton catalysis
Graphitic carbon nitride
Copper
Carbon dots
Mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of CDs on structure and property of Cu–C3N4

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns, (B) FTIR spectra and (C) Cu 2p XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4. (D) Low- and (E) high-magnification TEM images of Cu-g-C3N4. (F–I) STEM and elemental mapping images of Cu-g-C3N4.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, (B) Plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hν, and (C) Band structure alignments of g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4 (Excitation wavelength: 385 nm).

(A) Photodegradation of RhB by g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4 under visible light illumination. (B) Photoluminescence spectra of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid for •OH detection and (C) Absorption spectra of NBT for •O2 - detection of g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4. Photodegradation of RhB by (D) g-C3N4 and (E) Cu-g-C3N4 under visible light illumination in the presence of scavengers. (F) The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves of g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4.

(A) Fenton and photo-Fenton degradation of RhB by g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4 (catalyst = 0.4 mg/L, H2O2 = 4 mM, RhB = 10 mg/L). (B) K value of photo-, Fenton- and photo-Fenton catalysis by g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4. (C) Photo-Fenton degradation of RhB by Cu-g-C3N4 in the presence of scavengers. (D) The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves of Cu-g-C3N4 with/without H2O2.
(A) FTIR spectra and (B) Cu 2p XPS spectra of CDs/Cu–C3N4. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra and (D) Band structure alignments of CDs/Cu–C3N4.

(A) Fenton and photo-Fenton degradation of RhB by CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 (catalyst = 0.4 mg/L, H2O2 = 4 mM, RhB = 10 mg/L). (B) K value of photo-, Fenton- and photo-Fenton catalysis by CDs/Cu-g-C3N4. (C) Photoluminescence spectra of CDs in the presence of acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene and donor N,N-diethylaninline. (D) The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 with/without H2O2.
Schematic mechanism for the excellent photo-Fenton catalytic activity of as synthesized samples.

Cycling stability of (A) Cu-g-C3N4 and (B) CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 for photo-Fenton degradation of RhB. (C) Effect of pH on photo-Fenton degradation of RhB by Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4. Photo-Fenton degradation of (D) TC, (E) 4-NP and (F) MO by Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4.
Comparison of the catalytic activities of the as synthesized samples with other Fenton/photo-Fenton catalysts reported in literatures.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride modified with trace-level copper and carbon dots exhibits excellent photo-Fenton catalytic activity with low consumption of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a competent candidate for heterogeneous Fenton catalysis application. However, g-C3N4 based heterogeneous Fenton catalysts suffer from either high consumption of H2O2 or inefficient catalytic efficiency. In this work, g-C3N4 modified with trace-level copper and carbon dots (CDs) was designed. Contrary to previous understanding, the results revealed that the introduction of Cu decreased the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 owing to the trapping of photogenerated charge carriers by introduced Cu species. But delightedly, the g-C3N4 modified with trace-level Cu (Cu-g-C3N4) not only exhibited exceptional Fenton-like catalytic activity with low consumption of H2O2 (4 mM), but also showed favorable photo-Fenton synergistic catalytic activity. The excellent performance was fundamentally due to the introduced trace-level Cu that avoided producing excessive defects acting as trapping centers of active radicals and simultaneously reduced the consumption of photogenerated charge carriers by Cu species themselves, giving rise to more active radicals and highly efficient utilization of H2O2. Moreover, after introducing CDs, the obtained CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed higher photo-Fenton activity in degrading different organic pollutants compared to Cu-g-C3N4 owing to the role of CDs in facilitating the charge transfer and strengthening the interaction with organic pollutants. The constructed Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed wide application pH and high stability, holding great potential in practical pollutants degradation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fenton catalysis is considered to be one of the promising technologies in degrading organic pollutants for its ability to produce highly powerful hydroxyl radicals •OH [1,2]. Compared with traditional Fenton catalysts, heterogeneous Fenton catalysts have attracted increasing attention due to their advantages of wide working pH and good stability [3–8]. Recently, to further promote the Fenton catalytic performance, photo-Fenton catalysis has been proposed [9–13]. On the one hand, the photogenerated electrons could reduce the metal of high-valence state to active low-valence state and thus accelerate the cycling of M(n + m)+/Mn + which is the rate limiting step for the Fenton-like reaction. On the other hand, besides •OH, h+ and •O2 - as well as other active radicals generated by semiconductor under illumination can participate in degrading organic pollutants. Therefore, photo-Fenton catalysts show great potential for highly efficient degradation of environmental pollutants [14–17].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), with good thermal and chemical stability as well as unique physicochemical characteristics, has been demonstrated to be a competent candidate to integrate with other nanomaterials for constructing effective heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalysts [18–21]. Apart from the important role as support, recent studies have indicated that g-C3N4 itself after modification with metallic elements can exhibit heterogeneous Fenton-like catalytic activity by virtue of its unique electronic structure (rich long-pair electrons) to bind or anchoring heteroatoms [22–26]. Among the metallic elements, copper not only displays similar redox properties with iron which is most widely used for constructing Fenton or Fenton-like catalysts, but also possesses a significantly faster reaction constant with H2O2 (k(Cu+/H2O2) = 1.0 × 104 M-1s-1, k(Cu2+/H2O2) = 4.6 × 102 M-1s-1, k(Fe2+/H2O2) = 76 M-1s-1, k(Fe3+/H2O2) = 0.001–0.02 M-1s-1) [25–27], thus is considered to be a promising element to construct heterogeneous Fenton catalysts. Now the good Fenton-like catalytic property of copper modified g-C3N4 has been demonstrated, however, the desired photo-Fenton synergistic activity of the obtained Cu modified g-C3N4 was not observed and a high amount of H2O2 was needed (the weight ratio of H2O2 to catalyst was 1.7 and 51, respectively) [25,26]. As is known, g-C3N4 is an effective visible light photocatalyst in multifunctional usage of solar energy [28–32], it is logical to anticipate the photo-Fenton synergetic activity of g-C3N4 based Fenton-like catalysts. Thus, optimizing material design and in-depth understanding the photo-Fenton synergetic catalytic mechanism are highly needed to enhance the catalytic activity and utilization of H2O2 for highly efficient degradation of organic pollutants to address the environmental issues.

Here, g-C3N4 modified with only trace-level Cu (Cu-g-C3N4) was first designed and a systematic study on its photo-, Fenton-like and photo-Fenton catalytic performance was carried out to unveil the effect of Cu. Based on the unique physicochemical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) [33–38], trace-level Cu and CDs co-modified g-C3N4 was further constructed. It was found that the introduction of Cu did not improve but decreased the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 owing to the consumption of the photogenerated charge carriers caused by the self-redox of introduced Cu species. But delightedly, at an introduction of Cu with theoretical weight percentage of 1 wt%, Cu-g-C3N4 exhibited exceptional Fenton-like catalytic activity by consuming small amounts of H2O2 (4 mM, the weight ratio of H2O2 to catalyst was only 0.34). And the Fenton-like catalytic activity was further enhanced when introducing visible light illumination. The desirable photo-Fenton catalytic activity of Cu-g-C3N4 was mainly due to the introduced trace-level Cu which avoided producing lots of surface defects as trapping centers of active radicals and prevented excessive consumption of photogenerated charge carriers by Cu species themselves, giving rise to more active radicals and efficient utilization of H2O2. Additionally, due to that the introduction of CDs further facilitated charge transfer and tailored the surface states, the obtained CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed higher photo-Fenton activity in degrading different organic pollutants compared to Cu-g-C3N4. Furthermore, the developed Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 exhibited excellent photo-Fenton catalytic property within a wide pH range and high stability, indicating the potential for practical applications. This study offers new enlightenments in designing hybrid catalysts and shaping their performances.

The phase structure of Cu-g-C3N4 obtained from the thermal condensation of urea and copper chloride was identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 A, Cu-g-C3N4 showed similar patterns to that of g-C3N4. Due to the introduction of trace-level Cu, the intensity of the characteristic peak at 13o (in-plane stacking) and 27o (interfacial stacking) showed no obvious changes except the peak at 27o slightly shifted to the higher angle, indicating that a decrease of the interplanar stacking layers spacing due to the distortion of in-plane structural packing after Cu intercalation. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1B), compared with g-C3N4, weak peaks at 500 and 2150 cm-1 were observed for Cu-g-C3N4. The former was assigned to Cu–N stretching vibration, suggesting that introduced Cu species were coordinated with N atoms. The latter was assigned to C≡N groups, signifying that the introduction of Cu led to the generation of defects which tend to capture active radicals and decrease the catalytic activity [22]. Note that the peak intensity corresponding to C≡N in this study was significantly weaker compared to the literatures owing to the small amount of Cu dopant.

The surface composition and chemical states of the samples were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The high-resolution Cu 2p XPS spectrum demonstrated that dominated Cu species was Cu + according to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 peaks at 951 and 931.4 eV (Fig. 1C). Deconvolution of C1s spectra for g-C3N4 and Cu-g-C3N4 were identical (Fig. S1A), whereas the peaks in the N1s XPS spectrum shifted to higher energy position after the introduction of Cu, evidencing that Cu species bonded to the N atoms (Fig. S1B). In the O1s XPS spectra, no Cu–O bond was observed (Fig. S1C). These results indicated that the introduced Cu species were filled in the interstitial sites of heptazine rings and stabilized through coordinative Cu–N bonds.

Fig. 1D,E shows the morphology and microstructure of the samples investigated with TEM. Compared to the layered structure of g-C3N4 (Fig. S2), mesopores were clearly observed in the layered structure of Cu-g-C3N4. And the STEM and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images indicated that Cu species homogeneously distributed in the g-C3N4 framework (Fig. 1F–I). N2 adsorption-desorption measurements further confirmed the porous structures. As shown in Fig. S3, in comparison with g-C3N4 (59.53 m2 g-1), Cu-g-C3N4 showed a higher adsorption, suggesting an increased surface area (82.60 m2 g-1). In addition, the pore size distribution curves indicated that Cu-g-C3N4 possessed more mesopores. The increased surface areas would be beneficial for enhancing catalytic activity by providing more active sites [39].

The optical properties of the samples were evaluated by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy. The light absorption edge of Cu-g-C3N4 was shifted in the long wavelength range compared to that of g-C3N4 (Fig. 2 A), and the derived bandgap determined by Kubelka–Munk function was narrowed from 2.82 to 2.74 eV (Fig. 2B). Together with XPS valence band (VB) spectra (Fig. S4), the band alignments were obtained. As shown in Fig. 2C, the introduction of Cu altered the band structure alignments of g-C3N4 to some extent. The PL emission spectra in Fig. 2D showed that the peak intensity of Cu-g-C3N4 was much weaker than that of g-C3N4, implying an extremely reduced radiative recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This was further supported by the time resolved photoluminescence decay test (Fig. S5 and Table S1). The average lifetime for Cu-g-C3N4 (3.70 ns) was shorter than that of CDs (6.15 ns) at probed emission wavelength of 440 nm, confirming the improved charge carriers transport.

The photocatalytic performance of Cu-g-C3N4 was first investigated through degradation of RhB. Contrary to common understanding [30,40], the introduction of Cu decreased the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 (Fig. 3 A and Fig. S6). Such phenomenon is difficult to understand since the introduction of Cu improved the visible light absorption and increased the surface area as well as suppressed the radiative recombination of photogenerated charge carriers of g-C3N4, as revealed by above characterization. To unveil the underlying reason on the abnormal phenomenon, taken g-C3N4 modified with 1 wt% Cu into account, the generation of •OH and •O2 - radicals were first measured according to the reported method [41]. The results of monitoring •OH (Fig. 3B) and •O2 - (Fig. 3C) radicals showed a lower fluorescent signal and a higher absorption signal for Cu-g-C3N4 compared with g-C3N4, indicating less radicles generated by Cu-g-C3N4 which echoed the decreased photodegradtion activity. Additionally, trapping experiments were carried out and revealed that although the photodegradtion activity of both Cu-g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 showed similar trend in the presence of scavengers, the photodegradtion activities of Cu-g-C3N4 were weaker than that of g-C3N4 in all cases (Fig. 3D and E). The results suggested that the photogenerated h+ and e- mainly participated in other reaction rather than the degradation of RhB.

Based on the above findings, we proposed the following mechanism on the reduced photodegradtion activity of Cu-g-C3N4. The photogenerated e- partially reduced O2 to H2O2 due to the activation of Cu–N bonding to O2 [42]. The formed H2O2 subsequently interacted with Cu species and decomposed into O2, which was proved by the experimental phenomenon of the observation of bubbles during the photocatalytic reaction in the presence of Cu-g-C3N4. Besides, suggested by reference, [43] the photogenerated charge carriers could be captured by self-redox of Cu species. Therefore, fewer radicals were detected in Cu-g-C3N4 system. The conjecture was corroborated by the transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results. The photocurrent density of Cu-g-C3N4 was lower (Fig. 3F) and the radius of Nynquist curve was larger (Fig. S7) in comparison with g-C3N4, indicating that less amounts of charge carriers transferred to surface. The results clarified that the introduction of Cu inhibited the photodegradation activity of g-C3N4 due to the trapping of charge carriers by Cu species.

We next investigated the Fenton catalytic performance of the as-synthesized Cu-g-C3N4. Interestingly, Cu-g-C3N4 showed outstanding Fenton catalytic activity in the presence of very small amounts of H2O2 (4 mM) (Fig. 4 A, Fig. S8). The performance was much better than the previous reports which needed a large amount of H2O2 to realize high degradation rate [25,26]. The excellent Fenton-like activity of Cu-g-C3N4 in this work were mainly associated with the introduced trace-level Cu that effectively avoided producing excessive defects such as Cu≡N as revealed in Fig. 1B, leading to highly efficient utilization of H2O2 and significantly enhanced catalytic activity. More excitingly, the Fenton-like catalytic activity was further improved when introducing visible light illumination (Fig. 4A), i.e., the obtained Cu-g-C3N4 exhibited desirable photo-Fenton catalytic activity which was not observed in the literatures [25,26]. The pseudo-first-order kinetic plot was used to calculate the apparent rate constants (Fig. S9). The K value of photo-Fenton catalysis over Cu-g-C3N4 reached to 0.232 min-1, which was almost 1.4 and 28 times than that of Fenton catalysis and photocatalysis (Fig. 4B).

To clarify the possible mechanism for the favorable photo-Fenton catalytic activity, the effect of H2O2 under illumination condition was first evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4A, no degradation of RhB occurred by H2O2 under illumination in the absence of the catalysts, thus the enhanced activity owing to the decomposition of H2O2 triggered by illumination could be excluded. Then the trapping experiments in the presence of H2O2 were performed. It is seen that the photo-Fenton catalytic activity of g-C3N4 showed a significant decrease in the presence of •OH (TBA) and e- (NaIO3) scavenger and a slight decrease when bubbling N2 (•O2 - scavenger) but displayed enhancement in the presence of h+ scavenger (EDTA-2Na) (Fig. S10), indicating that •OH and e- were the main radicals for boosting the photo-Fenton catalytic activity. Significantly different from the observation for g-C3N4, it was found from Fig. 4C that the photo-Fenton catalytic activity of Cu-g-C3N4 not only dramatically decreased in the presence of •OH scavenger but also moderately decreased in the presence of h+ and e- scavenger, while the activity showed no obvious change when bubbling N2. This implied that radicals of •OH, h+ and e- together contributed to the excellent photo-Fenton activity of Cu-g-C3N4, which could be understood as follows. First, the oxidation of Cu+ by h+ in photocatalytic system was replaced by H2O2 in the photo-Fenton catalytic system due to the stronger oxidative ability of H2O2 than that of h+ [44]. This could bring two positive effects. One was that higher amount of •OH could be produced by the reaction between Cu+ and H2O2. This was supported by the detection test of •OH (Fig. S11), where stronger fluorescence signal for photo-Fenton reaction was observed than that for Fenton-like reaction. Another was that the uncaptured h+ by Cu+ participated degradation of RhB, which was proved by the inhibited activity in the presence of h+ scavenger (Fig. 4C). Second, given the strong reduction ability of electrons in the conduction band of Cu-g-C3N4, photogenerated e- could reduce Cu species and accelerate the cycling of CuII/CuI, which was validated by the lower photocurrent density of Cu-g-C3N4 in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 4D). Owing to the consumption of photogenerated e- by reduction of Cu species, plus the external addition of H2O2 could suppress the reduction of O2 to H2O2, the generation of radicals originated from O2 could be neglected, so no change of the catalytic ability was observed when bubbling N2. The above results highlighted the remarkable advantages of trace-level Cu modification in promoting the catalytic activity and utilization of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on structure and property of Cu–C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the unique feature of CDs, the trace-level Cu and CDs co-modified g-C3N4 was further synthesized by thermal condensation of urea and copper chloride in the presence of CDs with theoretical weight percentage of 0.001 wt%. The obtained CDs/Cu–C3N4 exhibited similar FTIR and XPS spectra with that of Cu–C3N4 (Fig. 5 A and B and Fig. S12), indicating that the introduction of CDs did not affect the main structure and chemical composition. However, it was observed that the peak positions of Cu in CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 were shifted a little compared to Cu-g-C3N4 (Fig. 5B), indicating the interaction between CDs and Cu–C3N4. For optical property, the introduction of CDs did not lead to obvious change of the absorption spectrum (Fig. 5C) but made conduction band moving down a little (Fig. 5D) which was derived from the calculated bandgap by Kubelka-Munk function and XPS valence band (VB) spectrum (Figs. S13A and B). And the weaker PL peak intensity and shorter PL lifetime of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 compared to Cu-g-C3N4 (Figs. S13C and D) demonstrated that the introduced CDs further promoted the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. In addition, the introduction of CDs led to higher surface areas of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 (98.54 m2 g-1) than that of Cu-g-C3N4 (82.60 m2 g-1), revealed by N2 adsorption-desorption measurements (Fig. S14).

The catalytic performance of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 was evaluated using the same procedure with Cu-g-C3N4. The photodegradation activity of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 was weaker than that of Cu-g-C3N4 (Fig. S15) although CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 exhibited higher surface area and faster charge transfer. Such phenomenon, which was different from the reported CDs modified g-C3N4 samples that showed a significant enhancement in photocatalytic activity after adding CDs [34], can be understood due to the feature of CDs as electron acceptor and donor (Fig. 6 C) that promoted the self-redox of Cu species, thus inhibiting the transfer of charge carriers to surface, which was confirmed by the lowest photocurrent density of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 under photocatalytic reaction condition (Fig. 6D). When introducing H2O2, CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 also showed slightly decreased Fenton-like catalytic activity in comparison to Cu-g-C3N4 (Fig. 6A). This could be explained by the fact that the introduced CDs might cover the Cu active sites. But interestingly, under visible light illumination, CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed higher photo-Fenton activity than that of Cu-g-C3N4, and the apparent rate constant reached to 0.243 min-1 (Fig. 6B). Based on the photo- and Fenton catalytic property, it can be drawn that the improved photo-Fenton catalytic activity was mainly attributed to the capability of CDs in facilitating charge transfer that compensated their negative effect to Cu active sits, which was supported by the highest photocurrent density of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 under photo-Fenton reaction condition (Fig. 6D). The performance of the developed Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 was comparable and even higher than the reported state-of-the-art material systems (Table 1 ). The schematic of the catalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 7 . It should be noted that for the not significant improvement resulted from CDs, further tailoring on the catalysts and more in-depth study are needed.

To access the stability and durability, the cycling test, effect of pH, adaptability to different pollutants were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 A and B, both Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed no obvious decrease of photo-Fenton catalytic activity towards RhB degradation after repeating five cycles. The used Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 retained its original composition and structure as revealed by XPS spectra (Figs. S16 and S17), indicating the excellent stability. Fig. 8C shows the effect of pH on the photo-Fenton degradation of RhB. It is seen that both Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 showed excellent photo-Fenton catalytic activity at an extensively wide pH range. At acidic pH, a significant decrease of activity was observed. This was related to two potential reasons. One was the intrinsic negative zeta potential of Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 (Fig. S18) that led to their protonation at acidic pH, giving rise to the electrostatic repulsion with cationic RhB. The other was the competition adsorption between H+ and cationic RhB. When increasing pH, the repulsion decreased and led to the increased degradation rate. The excellent catalytic performance in neutral pH is favorable for practical organic pollutants removal. Fig. 8D–F show the photo-Fenton degradation abilities of the samples to tetracycline (TC), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and anionic methyl orange (MO) at approximate neutral pH. It can be seen that CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 displayed higher photo-Fenton catalytic activity on degradation of these organic pollutants. This was attributed to the π-π interactions between CDs and organic pollutants that benefits to concentrate organic pollutants around the surface of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 [35]. Benefiting from the intriguing properties of CDs such as functional groups and better tenability, it is worth anticipating that the introduction of CDs could potentially lead CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 to a greater use in practical applications.

In this study g-C3N4 modified with trace-level Cu and CDs was constructed. It was demonstrated that the introduction of Cu decreased the photodegradation ability of g-C3N4 owing to the trapping of the photogenerated charge carriers caused by introduced Cu species. But excitingly, the g-C3N4 modified with trace-level Cu (Cu-g-C3N4) showed high Fenton catalytic activity by consuming only small amounts of H2O2. And the Fenton catalytic activity was further enhanced under visible light illumination. The excellent photo-Fenton catalytic activity was mainly attributed to the introduced trace-level Cu, which effectively prevented the generation of the excessive defects as trapping centers of active radicals and greatly reduced the consumption of photogenerated charge carriers by Cu species themselves, thus yielding more radicals and promoting utilization of H2O2. Moreover, introducing CDs could further promote the charge transfer and surface interaction, leading to the higher photo-Fenton activity of CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 in degrading different organic pollutants. The constructed Cu-g-C3N4 and CDs/Cu-g-C3N4 with excellent stability and durability could be promising catalysts for highly efficient degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the key research and development program of Shanxi Province (International Cooperation) (201803D421091, 201903D421082), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201901D111170, 20210302123029, 20210302124127), Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi (OIT), Specialized Research Fund for the Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, and the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020L0293).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2022.04.126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treatment of organic pollutants by homogeneous and heterogeneous Fenton reaction processes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Jain, A.K. Singh, H. Kim, E. Lichtfouse, V.K. Sharma, Treatment of organic pollutants by homogeneous and heterogeneous Fenton reaction processes, Environ. Chem. Lett. 16 (2018) 947-967.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies for enhancing the heterogeneous Fenton catalytic reactivity: a review
